clean_story: New skills stored.  Brain rewires itself. "Memory formation" is how your brain saves experiences—like a camera that develops its own film, then files it away for later. When you learn something new, neurons (tiny brain cells) talk to each other by sending electrical signals across gaps called synapses. Think of it like kids passing notes in class—except the notes make the gap smaller each time, so the next message travels faster. This strengthening process is called long-term potentiation—your brain’s way of bookmarking "important" moments. Example:Practicing a piano piece over and over forces those neuron paths to thicken, like a trail in the woods becoming a road. Sleep cements it—your brain replays the day’s events at 20x speed, locking them in. Stress or distraction?They’re like static on a radio, scrambling the signal before it saves. That’s why cramming rarely works—but spaced repetition (like flashcards over days) builds memory like brick by brick. Three related subjects are neuroplasticity rules, sleep’s hidden work, and forgetting’s purpose.
